title: Machine learning application for cell delay accuracy improvement at post-placement stage: A case study for combinational cells
abstract: In the early VLSI design stages, as most interest is given to timing closure, various techniques are deployed to satisfy the timing constraints and close design timing. Nevertheless, timing still needs to be accurate at the early stages due to the lack of physical design information. Therefore, the challenge for most EDA Logic Synthesis engines is to improve the overall timing accuracy at higher levels of abstraction. In this paper, we shall reduce the error gap in Cell Delay estimation for a placed gate-level netlist. We will generate the post-placed netlist from two different Siemens EDA tools. The purpose is to improve the accuracy of the cell delays of Oasys-RTL Synthesis tool, based on accurate cell delays from Aprisa PnR. We have trained and tested various Machine Learning models on several 16 nm designs. We achieved the highest accuracy using the Random Forest model, where the average accuracy is 91.25%. Furthermore, as the accuracy of the Cell delay is improved, the average Root-Mean-Square-Error (RMSE) is reduced from 16.958 to 4.469.
figure_captions: Fig. 2. Scattered plots for initial Oasys cell delay versus Aprisa cell delay.
Fig. 3. Scatter plots of predicted cell delay vs. Actual cell delay for each design.
Fig. 4. Scatter plots of predicted cell delay vs. Actual cell delay for TC5 using models trained on bunch dataset.
table_captions: Table 5 RMSE, MAE and R2 for models trained per design.
Table 6 Models RMSE, MAE and R2 for model trained using Bunch dataset.

Table 5 summarizes each model’s accuracy R2 score and the obtained error metrics RMSE and MAE. We note that the models’ response differs from one design to another depending on the internal design architecture. The Random Forest predictions are the most accurate, with an average accuracy score of 91.25%. The Random Forest RMSE and MAE as well are the lowest average error with an RMSE and MAE of 4.469 and 1.9, respectively. With a very close accuracy and lower RMSE and MAE, the KNN model has the second least average errors of 4.941 and 2.144 and the second-highest accuracy score of 89.28%. The Decision Tree also offers a high accuracy score of 87.49% and average errors of 5.212 and 2.529.
